notes: Foreign partner: Willys-Overland / Kaiser Jeep (USA); Iranian partner: Jafar Akhavan; ends/status: 1980; Founded 1956 importing Willys Jeeps; began local assembly of the 'Shahbaz' Jeep in 1959 (first Iranian-produced SUV); renamed Pars Khodro after 1979-80 nationalization of its later GM joint venture [source: WebSearch aggregation (Wikipedia/Grokipedia Pars Khodro)]

Foreign partner: Rootes Group (UK; became Chrysler UK 1967, then Talbot/Peugeot 1978) (United Kingdom); Iranian partner: Iran National Company (founded Aug 1962 by Ahmad and Mahmud Khayami, initial capital 100,000,000 Rials); ends/status: 2005; 1966: Rootes Group licensed Iran National to produce the Rootes Arrow (Hillman Hunter); production began 13 May 1967 at 6,000 units/yr, badged 'Paykan' (Persian for Arrow). Local content rose from near-zero to ~44% by 1979 and ~98% in later production. 1967-2005 total Paykan output exceeded 2.2 million units. Foreign partner ownership changed hands twice (Rootes to Chrysler UK 1967, Chrysler UK to Peugeot/renamed Talbot 1978) without Iran National losing domestic management control per Encyclopaedia Iranica [source: Encyclopaedia Iranica IRAN NATIONAL COMPANY; aronline.co.uk; Wikipedia Paykan; WebSearch aggregation]

Foreign partner: Daimler-Benz AG (West Germany); Iranian partner: Iran National Company; ends/status: 1979; Iran National ran a parallel license agreement with Mercedes-Benz of Germany for assembly of buses and minibuses (semi-knocked-down parts) alongside its Rootes/Chrysler passenger-car licence -- both licences held simultaneously by the same Iranian firm, illustrating the multi-partner-licensing model common in pre-1979 Iranian industrial JVs [source: Encyclopaedia Iranica IRAN NATIONAL COMPANY]

variant_label: Khawar Industrial Group (-> Iran Khodro Diesel)
notes: Foreign partner: Daimler-Benz AG (West Germany); Iranian partner: Iranian investors (name not identified this round); ends/status: ongoing (post-1999 merged into Iran Khodro Diesel); Separate Mercedes-Benz TRUCK assembly-under-license venture (distinct from the Iran National bus/minibus licence above), founded 1966 as Khawar Industrial Group; merged with Iran Khodro's bus/midibus lines in 1999 to form Iran Khodro Diesel, which continued Daimler licensing relationships (with interruptions/disputes under sanctions) well past the Islamic Revolution [source: WebSearch aggregation (Wikipedia/Grokipedia Iran Khodro Diesel)]

Foreign partner: General Motors (USA); Iranian partner: Sherkat-Sahami (Iranian holding company); ends/status: 1980; June 1972: GM signs deal forming General Motors Iran Ltd.; produced the Opel Commodore under license 1974-1976 badged 'Chevrolet Royale'. Nationalized after the 1979 revolution and renamed Pars Khodro in 1980, absorbing/succeeding the earlier Jeep Iran Trading Company lineage [source: WebSearch aggregation (Wikipedia/Grokipedia Pars Khodro)]
